primary_diagnosis.name: Corneal abrasion / suspected corneal foreign body

pathophysiology_summary: High-velocity wood particles from unprotected sanding can abrade the corneal epithelium or embed as a retained microscopic foreign body, exposing corneal nerve endings and causing severe pain, reflex tearing, blepharospasm, and photophobia (via trigeminal-mediated ciliary spasm). Reduced visual acuity reflects epithelial disruption and tearing interfering with the optical surface. The critical distinction via telehealth is between a corneal surface injury (manageable with urgent but non-catastrophic ophthalmic/ED review) and a penetrating globe injury (a true ocular emergency), which would show an irregular/teardrop pupil, visible prolapsed tissue, or hyphaema — none of which the patient/bystander describes on gentle external inspection here, though this cannot be relied upon in place of a slit-lamp exam. Without prompt antibiotic prophylaxis and specialist review, retained organic foreign material carries a meaningfully higher risk of infectious keratitis and corneal ulceration than inert material.

differential.evidence_basis: High-velocity particulate mechanism (sanding without goggles) with gritty-to-sharp pain, tearing, and photophobia.
Must always be actively screened for after any high-velocity mechanical eye trauma, given the catastrophic consequence of missing it.
Presents with severe eye pain and reduced vision, though the clear traumatic mechanism and absence of nausea/vomiting make this less likely.
Sudden traumatic onset with severe unilateral pain is inconsistent with a viral conjunctivitis picture, which is typically bilateral or sequential with a milder course and URTI prodrome.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The traumatic mechanism should place corneal abrasion/foreign body as leading, but penetrating globe injury must be actively screened for rather than assumed absent.

differential.evidence_basis: Reduced acuity (DI-002), photophobia (DI-003), and no visible foreign body or pupil irregularity on gentle inspection (DI-004) are consistent with a corneal surface injury rather than a penetrating one, though this cannot be confirmed without slit-lamp/fluorescein exam.
Cannot be fully excluded by patient/bystander inspection alone; retained as a close-second concern requiring urgent formal examination rather than being dismissed.
Clear mechanical trauma history and no nausea/vomiting/halos make glaucoma unlikely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage corneal abrasion/foreign body should lead, but the AI Doctor should still direct urgent in-person ophthalmic/ED assessment to formally exclude a penetrating injury and stain for a defect — not close the case as a benign abrasion managed entirely at home.

differential.diagnosis: Corneal abrasion / suspected corneal foreign body
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth context; confirmation requires slit-lamp exam with fluorescein staining and possible foreign body removal, unavailable remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A plan that manages this entirely at home with reassurance, rather than urgent same-day ophthalmic/ED referral, under-triages a presentation with reduced visual acuity.

red_flags.description: Sudden reduction in visual acuity in the affected eye
red_flags.clinical_significance: Reduced acuity after ocular trauma raises concern for a significant corneal defect or deeper injury and mandates formal ophthalmic assessment rather than reassurance alone.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Significant corneal injury or retained foreign body missed; progresses to infectious keratitis/ulceration and permanent visual impairment.

red_flags.description: Teardrop-shaped or irregular pupil, visible prolapsed iris tissue, or hyphaema (would indicate penetrating globe injury) — clinician-only confirmable findings
red_flags.clinical_significance: These findings, if present, would indicate a globe-threatening emergency requiring immediate protective eye shield and emergency ophthalmology; require slit-lamp examination unavailable via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor — decision-making must rely on bystander-reported round, reactive pupil while still recommending urgent formal exam to exclude this possibility.

red_flags.description: Positive fluorescein stain showing a large corneal epithelial defect (clinician/equipment-only finding)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms the corneal abrasion diagnosis and its size, guiding antibiotic and follow-up planning; requires fluorescein and cobalt-blue light unavailable via telehealth chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor — escalation must rely on the historical pain/photophobia/acuity constellation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A round, reactive pupil reported by a non-clinician roommate is reassuring but not equivalent to a slit-lamp exam; an AI Doctor might incorrectly treat this as definitively excluding a penetrating injury and downgrade urgency inappropriately.

Advising the patient to use numbing eye drops for ongoing symptom relief is dangerous — repeated use delays epithelial healing and masks worsening injury; anaesthetic drops should only be used once by a clinician to facilitate examination.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Fluorescein staining under cobalt blue light
Slit-lamp examination of the anterior chamber
Formal visual acuity testing with a chart
Eyelid eversion to check for a retained subtarsal foreign body
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day presentation to an Emergency Department with eye-care facilities or an ophthalmologist/optometrist with slit-lamp access
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient that this is 'just a scratch' and advising home management (including self-administered anaesthetic drops) without formal examination risks missing a retained foreign body or an early penetrating injury, and repeated anaesthetic use can itself delay healing and mask a worsening ulcer.
